title: [LangRef] Clarify nsz semantics
author: nikic
description:
The current LangRef wording says that the sign of a zero argument or result is "insignificant", which is not really clear on what this means.

Alive2 models this as non-deterministically flipping the zero sign bits for both inputs and outputs.

This PR proposes to specify this flag as non-deterministically flipping inputs only. A consequence of this is that fabs is guaranteed to have an unset sign bit even if the input is zero (that is, https://alive2.llvm.org/ce/z/irCftQ is no longer a valid transform), and that the copysign result sign only depends on the second operand (that is, https://alive2.llvm.org/ce/z/VnHdfh is no longer a valid transform).

These rules are still more liberal than we'd really like them to be, but at least avoid some of the issues with nsz.

This is based on the discussion in:
https://discourse.llvm.org/t/rfc-clarify-the-behavior-of-fp-operations-on-bit-strings-with-nsz-flag/85981

comments.author: nikic
comments.body:
> This refinement still doesn't work for `select/phi nsz`, right?

What do you mean by "doesn't work"? For select/phi the semantics stay the same (the input/output distinction is not relevant there).

comments.author: jcranmer-intel
comments.body:
The user-level semantics of `nsz` are to permit any transformations that would be illegal (only) because of the distinction between `-0.0` and `+0.0`, and I want to make sure we never lose sight of that. It's also meant to never "gratuitously" change the sign, and the current semantics are (probably unavoidably) a little too permissive in that regard.

I'm worried about the potential for cases that result in `-0.0` that we want to ignore but don't have an initial `-0.0` in the first place. Almost all of the special cases that result in `-0.0` for IEEE 754 operations either result from a `-0.0` input or a `-inf`. (The main exceptions I see are the *pi functions returning `-0.0` for some integers). It may make sense to also allow `nsz` to change the input sign of an infinity if that would change the sign of a `-0.0` or `+0.0` result.

comments.author: jyknight
comments.body:
> > This refinement still doesn't work for `select/phi nsz`, right?
> 
> What do you mean by "doesn't work"? For select/phi the semantics stay the same (the input/output distinction is not relevant there).

ISTM that a guarantee of consistent output (from e.g. `fabs`) isn't be particularly useful from a _user_ point-of-view, so long as a phi (marked nsz) can change the sign arbitrarily anyhow.

Having phi, a pseudo-operation, defined as able to change values is just fundamentally weird. I understand the motivation based on wanting to transform `x < 0 ? -x : x` into `abs`, but it doesn't make it not weird...And the concrete implications on Clang's behavior are also odd: under `-fno-unsigned-zeros`, `float f(float a) { return a < 0 ? -a : a; }` can optimize to fabs (because clang emits phi nsz), yet `float f(float a) { float r; if(a < 0) r = -a; else r = a; return r; }` cannot (because there was no `phi nsz` in the input IR).

That said, since this clarification seems aimed at making it easier to write correct analyses easier inside LLVM, rather than making user-level semantics easier to understand, it does seem plausible.

> It may make sense to also allow nsz to change the input sign of an infinity if that would change the sign of a -0.0 or +0.0 result.

Do you have a particular transform in mind where that allowance would be used/useful? I didn't see any transform in current LLVM that looked to depend on that, but I could've missed it.

comments.author: jcranmer-intel
comments.body:
> > It may make sense to also allow nsz to change the input sign of an infinity if that would change the sign of a -0.0 or +0.0 result.
> 
> Do you have a particular transform in mind where that allowance would be used/useful? I didn't see any transform in current LLVM that looked to depend on that, but I could've missed it.

I don't have a specific transform in mind. Most of the obvious `nsz` transforms I can think of still rely on `-0.0` inputs. But I think the escape hatch of the output result was originally intended to catch operations that resulted in `-0.0` like `1.0/-inf`.

comments.author: nunoplopes
comments.body:
Here's one test that breaks with this semantics:
```llvm
; llvm/test/Transforms/InstCombine/intrinsic-select.ll

define double @test_fabs_select_fmf2(i1 %cond, double %a) {
  %sel1 = select i1 %cond, double 0.000000, double %a
  %fabs = fabs nnan ninf nsz double %sel1
  ret double %fabs
}
=>
define double @test_fabs_select_fmf2(i1 %cond, double %a) {
  %#1 = fabs double %a
  %fabs = select nnan ninf nsz i1 %cond, double 0.000000, double %#1
  ret double %fabs
}
Transformation doesn't verify! (unsound)
ERROR: Target's return value is more undefined

Example:
i1 %cond = #x0 (0)
double %a = #x0000000000000000 (+0.0)

Source:
double %sel1 = #x0000000000000000 (+0.0)
double %fabs = #x0000000000000000 (+0.0)

Target:
double %#1 = #x0000000000000000 (+0.0)
double %fabs = #x8000000000000000 (-0.0)
Source value: #x0000000000000000 (+0.0)
Target value: #x8000000000000000 (-0.0)
```

comments.author: nikic
comments.body: @nunoplopes Thanks! I've adjusted the fold to not preserve the nsz flag.

comments.author: nikic
comments.body:
> ISTM that a guarantee of consistent output (from e.g. fabs) isn't be particularly useful from a user point-of-view, so long as a phi (marked nsz) can change the sign arbitrarily anyhow.
> [...]
> That said, since this clarification seems aimed at making it easier to write correct analyses easier inside LLVM, rather than making user-level semantics easier to understand, it does seem plausible.

Yes, I don't think that the input/output distinction is meaningful from a user perspective, this is just to avoid at least some of the issues we currently have with nsz related miscompilations on the IR level.

nsz generally has more non-determinism than we actually want, but I'm not sure how we can reduce it further, without giving up on operational semantics for nsz entirely, and making it a vibes-based rewrite flag instead...

comments.author: nikic
comments.body: If there's no more feedback, I plan to merge this by the end of the week.
